meta.notes: Ch. 9 acids/bases MCQ: the Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty (consistent with the note on cpbs-001 in questions.json), so these are marked verified-from-source-text and were checked by definition/calculation. DEDUPED against questions.json cpbs-001..004 (Bronsted-Lowry acid definition, conjugate base of HF, acidic pH<7, and pH of 4.1e-5) — those four Ch. 9 MCQ are intentionally NOT repeated here. Ch. 1 MCQ answers come from the rendered Ch. 16.1 answer key (source-answer-key). SKIPPED: Ch. 1 MCQ #6 (read the volume from the graduated-cylinder image) because it requires an un-capturable figure.

questions.stem: Which of the following molecules is a base?
questions.explanation: A Brønsted-Lowry base is a proton acceptor. Ammonia (NH3) accepts a proton to become NH4+, so it is a base. HCl, H2SO4, and CH3COOH (acetic acid) are all proton donors (acids).

hints.prompt: What is a Bronsted-Lowry base?
hints.rationale: A Bronsted-Lowry base accepts protons.

hints.prompt: Sulfuric acid, H2SO4, readily releases H+ ions in water. Does that make it an acid or a base?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neither an acid nor a base

hints.rationale: H2SO4 donates protons, so it is an acid.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are classic strong acids (proton donors)?
hints.rationale: HCl and H2SO4 are strong acids, so neither can be the base.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty; answer independently verified (definitional).

questions.stem: What is the conjugate acid of NH3?
questions.explanation: A conjugate acid is formed by adding a proton (H+). Adding H+ to NH3 gives the ammonium ion, NH4+.

hints.prompt: How do you form the conjugate acid of a species?
hints.rationale: A conjugate acid is the base plus one H+.

hints.prompt: Adding a proton to NH3 changes its hydrogen count and its charge by how much?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One more H atom and a +1 increase in charge

choices.label: B
choices.text: One fewer H atom and a -1 change in charge

choices.label: C
choices.text: One more H atom and a -1 change in charge

hints.rationale: Adding H+ adds one hydrogen and one unit of positive charge.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are negatively charged (from removing a proton), not a conjugate acid?
hints.rationale: A conjugate acid must gain H+ and become more positive, so the negatively charged species are impossible.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty; answer independently verified (definitional).

questions.stem: What is an example of an everyday base?
questions.explanation: Blood has a pH of about 7.4, which is slightly basic (above 7). Vinegar, coffee, and gastric juice are all acidic (pH below 7).

hints.prompt: On the pH scale, what range is basic?
hints.rationale: Basic solutions have a pH above 7.

hints.prompt: Gastric juice (stomach fluid) contains hydrochloric acid. Is it acidic or basic?
hints.rationale: Gastric juice contains HCl, which makes it strongly acidic.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated as clearly acidic (well below pH 7)?
hints.rationale: Vinegar (acetic acid) and gastric juice (hydrochloric acid) are strongly acidic.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty; answer independently verified (blood pH ~7.4 is basic).

questions.stem: What is the pOH of a solution with [H3O+] = 1.6 × 10^-8 M?
questions.explanation: First find pH = -log(1.6 × 10^-8) = 7.80. Then pOH = 14.00 - pH = 14.00 - 7.80 = 6.20. (Choice C, 7.80, is the pH itself—a trap.)

hints.prompt: How are pH and pOH related at 25 degrees C?
hints.rationale: At 25 degrees C, pH + pOH = 14.

hints.prompt: First find pH = -log[H3O+], then pOH = 14 - pH. Which setup is correct for [H3O+] = 1.6 x 10^-8?
hints.rationale: Compute pH with the negative log, then subtract from 14 to get pOH.

hints.prompt: Which choice is the trap equal to the solution's pH rather than its pOH?
hints.rationale: 7.80 = -log(1.6 x 10^-8) is the pH; the question asks for the pOH.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (exponent rendered with ^). The Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: What is the hydronium concentration in a solution whose pH is 4.82?
questions.explanation: [H3O+] = 10^-pH = 10^-4.82 = 1.5 × 10^-5 M. (Choice D, 6.6 × 10^-10 M, is the hydroxide concentration.)

hints.prompt: How do you get [H3O+] from a known pH?
hints.rationale: [H3O+] equals 10 raised to the negative pH.

hints.prompt: For pH 4.82, which expression gives [H3O+]?
Which choice is the trap that gives the hydroxide concentration instead of the hydronium concentration?

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (exponents rendered with ^; distractor A exponent inferred from lost-superscript text). The Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: What is the hydroxide concentration in a solution whose pOH is 1.75?

hints.prompt: How do you get [OH-] from a known pOH?
hints.rationale: [OH-] equals 10 raised to the negative pOH.

hints.prompt: For pOH 1.75, which expression gives [OH-]?
Which choice is the trap that gives the hydronium concentration instead of the hydroxide concentration?

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (exponents rendered with ^). The Ch. 16.9 answer-key page returned empty; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is false?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A law explains why something is true.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A law describes a series of experimental observations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A theory can be refuted by new experimental results.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unlike a hypothesis, experimental data is required to formulate a theory.

questions.explanation: A scientific law describes a consistent pattern of observations but does not explain why it occurs; explaining 'why' is the role of a theory. The other three statements are true.

hints.prompt: Can a well-established scientific theory be revised or overturned by new experimental evidence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes - theories are testable and can be refuted by new results

choices.label: B
choices.text: No - a theory is an absolute, permanent truth

choices.label: C
choices.text: No - theories never change once proposed

choices.label: D
choices.text: Theories are formed without any evidence

hints.rationale: Scientific theories are provisional and can be refuted or refined by new data.

hints.prompt: Which statement about laws and theories is TRUE (and so is not the false answer)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'A law describes a series of experimental observations'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A law explains why something is true'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Laws and theories are the same thing'

hints.rationale: A law does describe experimental observations - a true statement.

hints.prompt: Which two statements are clearly TRUE and can be eliminated as the 'false' answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'A law describes a series of experimental observations' and 'A theory can be refuted by new experimental results'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A law explains why something is true' and 'A law describes a series of experimental observations'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A theory can be refuted by new experimental results' and 'A law explains why something is true'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Experimental data is required to formulate a theory' and 'A law explains why something is true'

hints.rationale: Both accurately describe scientific laws and theories, so neither is the false statement.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q1 = a).

questions.stem: Which is a base unit of the SI system?
questions.explanation: The second is one of the seven SI base units. The SI base unit for mass is the kilogram (not the gram), while the liter and cubic centimeter are units of volume (derived from length).

hints.prompt: What is an SI base unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One of the seven fundamental SI units from which all other units are derived

choices.label: C
choices.text: A unit built by combining other units

hints.rationale: SI base units are the seven fundamental units of the system.

hints.prompt: Which of the listed quantities are volume units (which are derived, not base)?
hints.rationale: Liter and cubic centimeter measure volume, a derived quantity.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they measure volume (a derived quantity)?
hints.rationale: Liter and cubic centimeter are volume units derived from length, not base units.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q2 = d).

questions.stem: Which is a derived unit of the SI system?
questions.explanation: A derived unit is constructed from base units. The milliliter is a volume (length cubed), so it is derived. Kelvin, meter, and kilogram are all SI base units.

hints.prompt: What is a derived SI unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A unit built by combining base units

choices.label: B
choices.text: One of the seven fundamental base units

hints.rationale: Derived units are combinations of the base units.

hints.prompt: Is the kilogram a base unit or a derived unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A base unit - the SI base unit of mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not an SI unit at all

hints.rationale: The kilogram is the SI base unit of mass.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are SI base units, not derived units?
hints.rationale: Kelvin (temperature) and kilogram (mass) are base units.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q3 = c).

questions.stem: You measure the mass of a 1 kg object five times: 0.978 kg, 0.979 kg, 0.979 kg, 0.976 kg, 0.977 kg. Are your results...?
questions.explanation: The five readings agree closely with one another (all 0.976-0.979 kg), so they are precise. But they are consistently about 0.02 kg below the true value of 1 kg, so they are not accurate.

hints.prompt: What is the difference between precision and accuracy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Precision is agreement among the readings; accuracy is closeness to the true value

choices.label: B
choices.text: Precision is closeness to the true value; accuracy is agreement among readings

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither depends on the true value

hints.rationale: Precision is reproducibility; accuracy is closeness to the true value.

hints.prompt: The five readings cluster tightly (0.976-0.979 kg). What does that tight clustering show?
hints.rationale: Closely agreeing values indicate good precision.

hints.prompt: The readings are consistently about 0.02 kg too low. Which two choices can be eliminated because they claim the results are accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Accurate, but not precise' and 'Both accurate and precise'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Precise, but not accurate' and 'Neither accurate nor precise'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Accurate, but not precise' and 'Precise, but not accurate'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Both accurate and precise' and 'Neither accurate nor precise'

hints.rationale: A consistent offset from the true value means the results are not accurate, ruling out both 'accurate' options.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q4 = b).

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of eggs in a dozen

questions.explanation: A counted or defined quantity—such as exactly 12 eggs in a dozen—is exact and has infinite significant figures. Measured quantities (height, mass, length) always carry some uncertainty.

hints.prompt: Which kind of quantity is exact (having infinite significant figures)?
hints.rationale: Counted or defined values are exact; measurements carry uncertainty.

hints.prompt: Three of the choices are physical measurements. What do all measurements have that exact numbers lack?
hints.rationale: Measured values carry uncertainty, unlike exact counts.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are measured (and therefore uncertain) quantities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The height of a person and the mass of a dog

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of eggs in a dozen and the length of a table

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of eggs in a dozen and the mass of a dog

choices.label: D
choices.text: The height of a person and the number of eggs in a dozen

hints.rationale: Height and mass are measured values, so they are not exact.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q5 = a).

questions.stem: Which number has only 2 significant figures?
questions.explanation: Leading zeros are not significant, so 0.94 has 2 significant figures. 0.207 has 3, 6.23 has 3, and 1082 has 4.

hints.prompt: What is the rule for significant figures when a number has leading zeros?

choices.label: C
choices.text: All zeros in any number are significant

hints.rationale: Leading zeros merely set the decimal place and are not significant.

hints.prompt: How many significant figures does 0.207 have (a zero between nonzero digits counts)?
hints.rationale: The 2, the captive 0, and the 7 are all significant, giving three.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they each have THREE significant figures?
hints.rationale: 0.207 and 6.23 each contain three significant figures.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q7 = b).

questions.stem: What is the assumed uncertainty in the value 7.934 g?
questions.explanation: By convention the uncertainty lies in the last reported digit. The last digit of 7.934 g is in the thousandths place, so the assumed uncertainty is ±0.001 g.

hints.prompt: By convention, where does the uncertainty of a measurement lie?
hints.rationale: The uncertainty is in the final significant digit.

hints.prompt: In 7.934 g, which place value is the last digit in?
hints.rationale: The final digit, 4, is in the thousandths place.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are far coarser than the reported digits allow?
hints.rationale: A value written to the thousandths place is known far more precisely than +/-1 or +/-0.1 g.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q8 = d).

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a homogeneous mixture?
questions.explanation: Air is a homogeneous mixture of gases with uniform composition throughout. Chocolate chip ice cream, dirt, and concrete are heterogeneous mixtures with visibly non-uniform composition.

hints.prompt: What defines a homogeneous mixture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has a uniform composition throughout

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a single pure element

hints.rationale: Homogeneous mixtures are uniform throughout.

hints.prompt: Chocolate chip ice cream has visible chocolate chips embedded in the cream. Is it homogeneous or heterogeneous?
hints.rationale: Visible, separable chips make its composition non-uniform, i.e., heterogeneous.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated as clearly heterogeneous (non-uniform)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chocolate chip ice cream and dirt

choices.label: D
choices.text: Concrete and chocolate chip ice cream

hints.rationale: Chocolate chip ice cream and dirt have visibly separate components.

questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 1 Scientific Method, Matter, and Measurements
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.1 MCQ: Q9 = a).

questions.stem: Which of the following is a mixture?
questions.explanation: Brass is a mixture (an alloy of copper and zinc). Helium, copper, and iron are pure substances (each is a single element).

hints.prompt: What distinguishes a mixture from a pure substance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A mixture combines two or more substances physically, with variable composition

choices.label: B
choices.text: A mixture is a single element

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mixture is always a compound

choices.label: D
choices.text: A mixture has a fixed number of atoms per formula

hints.rationale: Mixtures contain more than one substance in variable proportions.

hints.prompt: Helium gas consists only of helium atoms. Is it a mixture or a pure substance?
hints.rationale: Helium is a single element, so it is a pure substance, not a mixture.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are pure elements, not mixtures?
hints.rationale: Helium and iron are single elements, so neither is a mixture.
